advantages: Explicit metrics make neighbor and cluster structure interpretable—you can inspect which points are close and why.
Minkowski family (p=1,2,…) gives a single tunable knob to match axis-aligned vs radial geometry in k-NN.
Cosine similarity aligns with embedding semantics when magnitude is uninformative, improving retrieval quality.
Library support (scikit-learn `metric`, vector DB cosine indexes) makes swapping metrics cheap during experimentation.
disadvantages: No default metric fits all data; unscaled heterogeneous features make Euclidean rankings misleading.
High-dimensional spaces weaken distance contrast (curse of dimensionality)—many pairs look equally far under L2.
Cosine ignores magnitude, which hurts when scale carries signal (e.g. unnormalized counts that reflect importance).
Some algorithms assume a fixed geometry (k-means ↔ Euclidean); using the wrong metric requires a different algorithm, not just a parameter change.
